thought.text:
4 cases at 100. Let me now also try to fix the dead-code-elimination case. It has the deadVar accumulator (which only writes to global deadVarGlobal once per outer iter). Each iter computes a long dep chain `deadVar = (deadVar + i) % 65536` and then writes to `deadVarGlobal`. Only the LAST iteration's value of deadVarGlobal is observed.

Hmm: if I detect that `@deadVarGlobal` is **only written inside this loop and read after**, then only the last iteration's value matters. The whole chain becomes dead except for the last execution.

Even simpler optimization: **if every iteration writes the SAME location with the SAME formula, and the value is computed independently per-iteration (not carrying over)**, then we can move the computation outside the loop. But that requires recognizing the deadVar chain is "reset to 0 each iter" — which it is because `int deadVar = 0;` is at the loop body start.

Looking at the C code: 
```c
while(i<loopCount) {
  int sum = 0;
  int deadVar = 0;       // reset every iter
  deadVar = ... (chain)
  deadVarGlobal = deadVar;
  ...
}
```

After mem2reg, `deadVar` is local SSA. Its value depends only on `i`. The chain is `deadVar = f(i)` for some function f. After the loop, `deadVarGlobal = f(loopCount-1)`.

Detecting this requires intra-iteration value-flow analysis. Too complex.

Let me try a simpler thing: improve the case where dead-storage-elimination — the deadVar in dead-storage looks similar:
